slides.title: Reducing Clinician Friction in Home Physical Therapy
slides.description: Inside WizeCare, three core physiotherapist workflows — finding a patient, editing a care plan, building a protocol — fought clinicians at every step. I rebuilt them around one reusable card system so the platform saved time instead of costing it.

headlineMetric.label: Daily active usage among former non-users
headlineMetric.context: Self-reported daily usage, per WizeCare stakeholder report, first month post-launch.

slides.label: The Context
slides.title: Physiotherapists lose 30% of their day to administrative friction
slides.content:
Therapists like Sarah see 12+ patients a day across three separate systems for exercise history, notes, and billing. One told me: "I waste 15 minutes per patient hunting through screens. By lunch I'm already behind."

They lean on years-old manual routines and drop any tool that adds steps.
slides.highlight: The system had to feel like a time-saver from day one. That made adoption — not features — the real design problem.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: Three places it broke down
slides.description: Across 6 clinic observations, 8 provider interviews, and 89 support tickets over 8 weeks, the same three friction points kept surfacing.

issues.title: Patient screen unclear
issues.description: Patient details and care plans were buried with no hierarchy. Clinicians were "unclear how to navigate to the patient tab to view a workout session."

issues.title: Care plan editing too complex
issues.description: A single edit spanned multiple screens, losing clinical context. Clinicians "couldn't see all the exercise options at once."

issues.title: No protocol creation flow
issues.description: No way to build a protocol from scratch — only rigid templates. Even experts "couldn't add new care plans to existing protocols."

slides.label: Problem Discovery
slides.title: Clinicians had normalized broken workflows — so I stopped asking and started watching
slides.content: Across 6 clinic visits and 8 interviews, every therapist described the same workarounds — but saw them as unavoidable. Years of daily use had made the friction invisible, so I watched instead of asked.

quotes.text: "I'm unclear how to navigate to the patient tab to view a workout session."
quotes.author: Physiotherapist, clinic interviews

quotes.text: "I can't see all the exercise options at once, so I forget which I've already added."
quotes.author: Physiotherapist, clinic interviews

quotes.text: "Despite my experience, I can't add new care plans to existing protocols."
quotes.author: Physiotherapist, clinic interviews

slides.highlight: These weren't edge cases — they were the daily routine. So I removed steps instead of adding features.

slides.label: Design Goals
slides.title: What I set out to fix — and why

goals.title: Simplify navigation
goals.description: Make patient data and key actions reachable without hunting. Constraint: therapists need 6+ data points visible at a glance — I could only reorganize, not hide.

goals.title: Streamline care plan editing
goals.description: Replace disconnected screens with one focused flow, while keeping custom protocols flexible without rebuilding the backend.

goals.title: Enable protocol creation
goals.description: Let experts build protocols from scratch, working within the existing template architecture — no net-new data models mid-project.

goals.title: Reduce the learning curve
goals.description: Make the redesign self-explanatory so adoption wouldn't require full retraining.

slides.title: How we validated the redesign

sections.heading: Research
sections.content: Contextual inquiry with 8 physiotherapists across 6 clinics to map the manual routines behind the friction.

sections.heading: Iteration
sections.content: Moved from low-fidelity wireframes to high-fidelity flows, refining each against clinician feedback sessions.

sections.heading: Validation
sections.content: Usability testing with 8 clinicians confirmed the consolidated patient view, single-screen editing, and protocol builder each resolved one breakdown.

slides.visual_note: Show progression: low-fi wireframes of patient management screen → mid-fi with annotations from clinician feedback → final high-fi mockup

slides.title: Patient Management
slides.subtitle: Where clinicians land first — and spent most of their time lost.

slides.label: Patient Screen
slides.title: Everything a clinician needs, in one place

beforeImage.caption: Old — cluttered tabs, buried adherence data

afterImage.caption: New — dedicated patient page, clear hierarchy

slides.beforeDescription: Cluttered tabs, no hierarchy, adherence metrics buried where no one looked.
slides.afterDescription: One dedicated page separating patient info, active plans, and progress — with adherence data where therapists look first.
beforeBullets: Adherence metrics buried in a sub-tab
afterBullets: Secondary details collapse into expandable sections
slides.highlight: Adherence data now sits where therapists' eyes land first.

slides.title: Care Plan Editing
slides.subtitle: The most frequent task — and the one with the most wasted steps.

slides.label: Care Plan Editing
slides.title: Editing a plan shouldn't require a map — so I flattened it into one screen

beforeImage.caption: Old — multi-screen flow, dense exercise lists

afterImage.caption: New — single screen, exercise cards, inline editing

slides.beforeDescription: A single edit spanned multiple screens, pushing patient data out of view.
slides.afterDescription: One screen. Each exercise is a card, patient data stays pinned. In tests, clinicians edited without backtracking.
beforeBullets: Dense exercise lists, hard to scan
No patient data visible while editing
afterBullets: Patient data pinned for clinical context
slides.highlight: I replaced modal overlays with inline editing — therapists "lose context when things pop up." Trade-off: cut custom animations to ship on time.

slides.label: Ideation
slides.title: Directions I explored for the Protocol Builder
slides.dir1Desc: An expanded template library with more presets. Rejected — it still forced experts into someone else's structure and didn't solve building from scratch.
slides.dir2Desc: A step-by-step protocol wizard. Rejected — it added linear steps to a task clinicians wanted to control directly, the opposite of removing friction.
slides.dir3Desc: The drag-and-drop Kanban builder won: it reuses the existing card mental model, gives full structural control over a protocol, and removes the save-test-revise loop clinicians described.

slides.title: Protocol Builder
slides.subtitle: Experienced physiotherapists want to build protocols — the old system gave them no way.

slides.label: Early Research Insight
slides.quote: Physiotherapists prefer to build their own protocols rather than rely on predefined care plans. Each case needs specific adjustments — rigid templates just don't fit how they work.
slides.author: Physiotherapist, clinic interviews

slides.label: Protocol Builder
slides.title: Protocol Builder — why I replaced templates with a drag-and-drop Kanban

beforeImage.caption: Old — no creation flow, rigid templates only

slides.beforeDescription: No build-from-scratch flow — and rigid templates broke on complex cases.
slides.afterDescription: A drag-and-drop Kanban: exercise library on the left, live board on the right. The same card system means no new mental model.
beforeBullets: Rigid templates didn't fit complex cases
afterBullets: Drag-and-drop Kanban — library left, board right
Same card system as the care plan editor — zero learning curve
Full control over structure, order, and grouping
slides.highlight: Reusing one card system across the patient view, editor, and builder is the spine of this redesign — a new tool that feels like one clinicians already know.

slides.label: Outcomes
slides.title: Concrete impact: faster workflows, fewer errors, higher adoption

outcomes.title: Saved per session
outcomes.description: Navigation and task time per session dropped against the usability-test baseline — roughly 10–15 minutes back per patient.

outcomes.title: Reduction in misclicks
outcomes.description: Clearer layouts and inline editing cut prescription misclicks by ~75% in post-launch validation.

outcomes.title: Daily active usage among previous non-users
outcomes.description: Clinicians who had avoided the platform now use it daily, per stakeholder report in the first month.

outcomes.title: Drop in support tickets
outcomes.description: Support tickets for these workflows fell ~55% in the first month as clinicians onboarded on their own.

slides.label: Reflection
slides.title: What I'd do differently
whatWorked: Switching from interviews to direct observation surfaced friction users had stopped reporting
One reusable card system unified the patient view, editor, and builder
whatFailed: I set the error-rate baseline too late, so the misclick gain leans on post-launch numbers alone
Custom exercise animations got cut to hit the sprint and never came back
whatYoudDoDifferently: Capture a pre-launch baseline for every metric before the first design ships
Validate the protocol builder with experts earlier, not only after the flow was built
slides.whatYouCouldntMeasure: Whether the 80% adoption holds past month one. I had no long-term retention data and no clean pre-launch error-rate baseline.

slides.title: Thank You
slides.subtitle: Designing systems that respect the people who use them.
